topic: movie rec — wants something like Arrival

turns.content: I want a movie recommendation. I rewatched Arrival last night and now I'm in my feelings and I want more movies that hit like that. do you know what I mean

I know exactly what you mean, and Arrival is a perfect reference point because it has such a specific feeling — slow, quiet, intellectually serious, but also emotionally devastating in a way you don't see coming. That combination of "makes you think" and "makes you feel" is rare.

Before I recommend: is it more the sci-fi first-contact mystery aspect that hit you, the linguistic/cognition angle, the non-linear time structure, or the emotional gut-punch of the ending? Because different films do each of those things differently, and knowing which part wrecked you helps me aim.

it's the combination of the big conceptual idea (language shaping perception of time) and the emotional devastation. like the science-y stuff makes the emotional hit land harder because you actually believe the world. if that makes sense

That makes complete sense — it's the specificity of the idea that earns the emotional resonance. When the rules of the world are real and internally consistent, the feelings they generate feel real too. That's a very particular kind of science fiction and not all of it gets there.

Here are recommendations that I think actually hit that specific note:

**Almost certainly:**
- **Annihilation (2018)** — same director (Denis Villeneuve) as Arrival, no wait, different director (Alex Garland), but similar DNA. Strange, slow, beautiful, has a big idea at its core, ends in a way that lingers. Emotionally hits different than you expect.
- **Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind (2004)** — completely different genre but the same thing: a conceptual premise (erasing memories) earns an emotional devastation that wouldn't land without it.
- **Coherence (2013)** — tiny budget, huge idea. Shot in one night at a dinner party. The concept is a gut punch once it unfolds.

**Strong contenders:**
- **Interstellar (2014)** — bigger and louder than Arrival but the relativity/time stuff and the father-daughter emotional core are very much in the same family
- **Contact (1997)** — older, slower, gets at the same first-contact-and-what-it-means-for-humanity feeling
- **Upstream Color (2013)** — experimental and strange, but if you're patient it rewards you with something like what you're describing

Have you seen any of these already?

I've seen eternal sunshine, interstellar, and annihilation. loved all three. haven't seen the others

also — correction! Denis Villeneuve directed Arrival, not Annihilation. Annihilation is Alex Garland. you caught yourself but just in case lol

You're right and thank you — I did catch myself mid-sentence and managed to contradict myself anyway, which is impressive. Denis Villeneuve: Arrival (and Blade Runner 2049, Dune). Alex Garland: Annihilation (and Ex Machina, which is also on my list for you). Noted.

Okay, since you've covered the big three, here's where I'd start from what's left:

**Watch Coherence first** — it's only 90 minutes, it costs nothing to find, and if you like puzzle-box films that are also about people and relationships falling apart under pressure, it's going to mess you up in the best way. The concept is quantum physics applied to a dinner party. It's so good on a rewatch once you know what's happening.

**Then Ex Machina (Alex Garland, 2014)** — I should have mentioned this alongside Annihilation. Same patient, ideas-driven, quietly devastating structure. The ending particularly. It's about AI consciousness and what it means to truly think and feel, but it doesn't feel like a tech-bro film — it feels like a thriller about personhood.

**Then Contact** — it's slower and more earnest than the others, but if you can meet it where it is, the emotional payoff (especially the ending) is genuinely moving. Jodie Foster is incredible in it.

Does Coherence sound like a Friday night or a Saturday "I want to commit to something" movie to you?

Friday night for sure since it's short. I can do Ex Machina Saturday if I get Coherence out of the way

I've been meaning to watch Ex Machina for ages, people keep telling me to. does it hold up? it's from 2014 so I wasn't sure

Ex Machina holds up really well — possibly better now than in 2014 because the conversation around AI consciousness and what it means for machines to have genuine inner experience has only gotten more urgent and real-feeling. When it came out it felt like speculative fiction. Now it feels like it's asking questions that are live.

The things that haven't aged: the performances (Oscar Isaac is doing something strange and great, Alicia Vikander is phenomenal), the visual design of Ava, the philosophical questions it's wrestling with, the structure of the thriller. None of that dates.

The one mild caveat: it does have a slightly "2014 indie sci-fi" slowness in the first act that some people find it hard to get into. But if Arrival didn't feel too slow to you, Ex Machina won't either — you're clearly the target audience.

Go in knowing as little as possible. Don't read anything about the ending.
I already know vaguely how it ends because the internet but I'll try to forget lol. do you have anything else in the queue after those two in case I blow through them and want more

Always good to have a queue. Here's a deeper bench in roughly the same emotional and thematic territory:

**Still sci-fi, same vibe:**
- **Under the Skin (2013)** — Scarlett Johansson, almost no dialogue, deeply strange and slow and beautiful. It's about what it means to be human from the outside looking in. Genuinely unsettling.
- **Blade Runner 2049 (2017)** — Denis Villeneuve again, feels like a companion piece to Arrival in terms of pacing and scale. Long but earns every minute. The central question (what is real, what is memory) is right in your wheelhouse.
- **Moon (2009)** — Sam Rockwell alone on a moon base. Small movie, giant emotional idea. Cried at this one.

**Adjacent (not sci-fi but same emotional math):**
- **Never Let Me Go (2010)** — based on the Kazuo Ishiguro novel. Devastating. A big idea earns an emotional gut punch. Fair warning: it is very sad.
- **Synecdoche, New York (2008)** — Charlie Kaufman, who wrote Eternal Sunshine. This one is harder to get into but if it lands for you it lands hard. It's about mortality and the impossibility of capturing life. Not for everyone.

Blade Runner 2049 might be the strongest bet from this list given you've already loved the Villeneuve + Eternal Sunshine + Annihilation combination. Have you seen it?

I've seen blade runner 2049 and loved it. haven't seen any of the others. Moon sounds really good actually, I'm adding that

also Never Let Me Go — is it the Ishiguro adaptation? I read the book and cried so I'm scared of the movie lol

Yes, it's the Ishiguro adaptation — 2010, directed by Mark Romanek, with Carey Mulligan, Keira Knightley, and Andrew Garfield. It's very faithful to the book, which means yes, it will wreck you in the same way the book did. Probably more, because seeing the actors' faces doing the thing that the characters do in that final scene... Carey Mulligan especially.

If you cried at the book, give yourself a night when you're emotionally prepared for it. It's worth watching but maybe don't do it after an already emotionally heavy Friday night Coherence session.

**Moon is a great call for your queue** — it's quiet and character-driven and the idea at the center is the kind of thing you turn over in your head afterward. Very in the spirit of what you're looking for. Sam Rockwell does so much work with so little. And it was made on a tiny budget which makes it even more impressive.

You've got a solid lineup now: Coherence → Ex Machina → Moon → Under the Skin → Never Let Me Go (when emotionally ready). That should keep you busy for a few weeks.

Any genres or styles you want to avoid? Like is full horror off the table, or are you fine with tense/scary as long as it's more psychological than gory?

psychological is totally fine, gory is not really my thing. I can handle tense and unsettling, just not like... gratuitous

this list is perfect. I'm going to start with Coherence tonight actually, not even waiting for friday

Coherence on a Thursday night, impulsive and correct. That's the right energy for it — it's the kind of movie that benefits from going in without too much planning or expectation. Just put it on.

For what it's worth: watch it alone in the dark, no phone, no pausing to check what's happening. Let it be disorienting. The confusion is intentional and part of the experience. By the last 20 minutes you'll understand everything and you'll want to rewatch the first 20 minutes immediately.

All the other recommendations stay in the "psychological and unsettling but not gory" lane — you're safe with everything on that list.

Enjoy the movie. Come back devastated.
